figures.caption: Table 1: Main results on three Amazon-review datasets. R@k and N@k denote Recall@k and NDCG@k, respectively. The best result in each column is bolded and the second best is underlined.

Figure 1: Motivating failure mode of outcome-reward GRPO across three recommendation datasets. Here, exact-match feedback gives a reward of 3. GRPO typically improves reward early but later plateaus or becomes unstable as response length change and KL to the reference increase. The reward sparsity makes the instability of rollout-level credit assignment.

Figure 6: Prompt template used for Stage 3 RL training. Placeholders in ⟨·⟩ are filled with perexample content: ⟨history_sid_list⟩ is a comma-separated list of SID tuples drawn from the user’s interaction history, and the expected response is K=3 <think>. . .</think> thinking blocks (one per codebook level, matching the decoding layout of Eq. (4)) followed by the K=3 SID tokens for the predicted next item. Constrained decoding over the learned SID trie guarantees that every sampled SID is a valid item under the per-category codebook.

Figure 5: Case study on Industrial-and-Scientific. Both methods receive the same prompt, with SID tokens highlighted in red. Outcome GRPO follows the general user interest but misses the target, whereas SAPO identifies more discriminative evidence and predicts the correct SID.

Table 6: Hyperparameters per training stage for the Qwen3-1.7B backbone. All stages use AdamW in BF16 with 4 GPUs. Early stopping on validation loss (patience 3) is active in Stages 1–2. Stage 3 follows the group-relative surrogate of Eq. (9) with the rollout parameters shown.

Figure 7: A concrete prompt instance from Office-Products. The user’s history is represented entirely as SID tuples, and the rollout generates K=3 <think>. . .</think> thinking blocks (one per codebook level, matching the decoding layout of Eq. (4)) followed by the K=3 SID tokens. In this example the rollout recovers the ground-truth target exactly (all three levels correct); see Section 5.5 and Appendix N for side-by-side comparisons with outcome-reward rollouts under the same prompt. The per-step match reward of Eq. (5) is non-zero at every codebook level for this rollout (all three per-step match indicators fire), which is exactly the per-step match signal SAPO exploits.

Figure 2: Overview of SAPO. Stage 1 aligns the language model to the SID vocabulary, Stage 2 activates level-aware reasoning, and Stage 3 applies step-aligned reinforcement learning. Under the released decoding layout, the policy generates K thinking blocks followed by the K SID tokens of the predicted item. SAPO assigns a reasoning-step match reward using SID-token correctness, attaches a small format bonus at the last SID-token position, computes a reasoning-step group-relative advantage, and optimizes a surrogate with step-normalized token aggregation.
